body:
### Problem

I wasn't able to find any issues that seemed to specifically address this use-case.

As far as I can tell, `PackChanged` events are only sent when plugins change the state on disk. I'm finding that limiting in adding behaviour around vim.pack.add.

I'm trying to use some custom property to declare dependencies. I don't want to automatically install those dependencies - I only want to throw a warning or error if it's not missing. This is mostly useful in setups where vim.pack.add calls are made in one plugin per file in `~/.config/nvim/plugin/*.lua` (and a plugin is a dependency for multiple other plugins).

If an event were fired for every vim.pack.add call - it would be simple to write a simple snippet to:
1. Check for `data.dependencies`
2. `pcall(vim.pack.get, data.dependencies)`
3. Give a warning / error when `pcall` failed.

















### Expected behavior

Enable features _around_ vim.pack events which are not possible only during initial installs.

comment_data.body:
there is : 

- https://github.com/neovim/neovim/issues/26427

> If an event were fired for every vim.pack.add call

if you want to hook into function calls, that sounds more like: 

- https://github.com/neovim/neovim/pull/38488

> I'm trying to use some custom property to declare dependencies. I don't want to automatically install those dependencies - I only want to throw a warning or error if it's not missing.

The recommended solution *right now* is to use function `opts.load`. This *is* the way to execute code on every plugin load. The downside is that it requires explicit `vim.cmd.packadd()` call at the end, but I think it is a reasonable compromise.

The planned *long term* solutions are:
- #26427, although it is not *quite* the same as "on every plugin load", see [this comment](https://github.com/neovim/neovim/issues/26427#issuecomment-3355769227).
- #34778, since my current idea of supporting dependencies is exactly as described in this issue: check `pkg.json` for dependencies and warn/error if they are not active. This avoids complications of automatic transitive install and checkout of plugin dependencies.

---

I think this is safe to close as this is already possible and better solutions are tracked elsewhere.

> there is :
> 
>     * [:packadd event (autocmd) #26427](https://github.com/neovim/neovim/issues/26427)
> 
> 
> > If an event were fired for every vim.pack.add call
> 
> if you want to hook into function calls, that sounds more like:
> 
>     * [feat(lua): vim.on_fun() #38488](https://github.com/neovim/neovim/pull/38488)

From what I can tell, `OptionSet`, `SourcePre` or `SourcePost` events would not have any mechanism to get the plugin options. I guess it could be used if I wanted to hard-code the expected file paths - but that seems a bit round-about IMO.

As for `vim.on_fun()`, that looks like a pretty decent general purpose solution - and also seems like a very useful feature. I'd have to actually look at what `vim.pack.add` calls to see if there is even an appropriate function that could be used. One of the approaches I was taking a look at was along the lines of
```lua
local default_packadd = vim.pack.add
vim.pack.add = function(packs)
  -- logic here
  return default_packadd(packs)
end
```
But moved away from trying that, since it would be pretty annoying to deal with situations where a plugin and it's dependency are both added in the same `vim.pack.add` call. I'd have to handle both "does the plugin exist" as well as "will the plugin exist". `vim.on_fun` would have the same challenges here.

> > I'm trying to use some custom property to declare dependencies. I don't want to automatically install those dependencies - I only want to throw a warning or error if it's not missing.
> 
> The recommended solution _right now_ is to use function `opts.load`. This _is_ the way to execute code on every plugin load. The downside is that it requires explicit `vim.cmd.packadd()` call at the end, but I think it is a reasonable compromise.
> 
> The planned _long term_ solutions are:
> 
>     * [:packadd event (autocmd) #26427](https://github.com/neovim/neovim/issues/26427), although it is not _quite_ the same as "on every plugin load", see [this comment](https://github.com/neovim/neovim/issues/26427#issuecomment-3355769227).
> 
>     * [Add `packspec` support to `vim.pack` #34778](https://github.com/neovim/neovim/issues/34778), since my current idea of supporting dependencies is exactly as described in this issue: check `pkg.json` for dependencies and warn/error if they are not active. This avoids complications of automatic transitive install and checkout of plugin dependencies.
> 
> 
> I think this is safe to close as this is already possible and better solutions are tracked elsewhere.

I didn't see documentation for `opts.load`. I saw that lz.n uses that approach. I didn't want to use an undocumented method to do what I want just because it _happens_ to work now. But also I expect I'll use lz.n at some point in the future, and creating a function to wrap lz.n's function which replaces the default plugin behaviour started to feel a bit convoluted for what I was trying to achieve.

As for https://github.com/neovim/neovim/issues/34778, if your expected strategy for dependencies is close to what I'm describing - I'm happy to close this ticket, and I'll watch 34778. But I think I'm going to need to re-read the thread a bit. At quick glance however, I do think there may still be value in events. packspec would likely only address one type of dependency (lua package dependencies) as a boolean (present or not). But there are other use-cases for dependencies. There are optional dependencies (e.g. nvim-jdtls does not require nvim-dap, but as a user of nvim-jdtls, I may want to express that I want nvim-dap to be available first). There's also dependencies on executable available in a PATH, or environment variables.

Anyway - if this seems out of scope, I'm happy to close the issue.

> I didn't see documentation for `opts.load`. I saw that lz.n uses that approach. I didn't want to use an undocumented method to do what I want just because it _happens_ to work now.

It doesn't _happen to work now_, this is a [documented way of doing custom loading](https://github.com/neovim/neovim/blob/cbe142d3a4cd0a33b68d316d8cda26b68de75399/runtime/doc/pack.txt#L445-L447). So if you want to tweak how loading is done, use supply function `opts.load`. No need for any external dependencies if you don't want them.

labels.description: Need comments/opinions from people familiar with this topic.
labels.name: needs:discussion

title: Generate more events from vim.pack

type.description: A request, idea, or new functionality
type.name: Enhancement
